name: Cova del Dimoni
starting_point: Porto Cristo, Sa Punta Rassa street
access: Any bus line going to Porto Cristo
equipment: Helmet and lighting, neoprene wetsuit for navigating the main hall, and a small rope in case the ones usually installed have been removed.
description: An EU-protected cave (LIC) beneath the cliffs near Porto Cristo that is both a terrestrial and marine cave, popular with divers. The main hall measures 41x21 m with 8+ m ceiling height and is mostly flooded by a brackish lake fed through an underwater siphon. A magnificent large column dominates the hall, slightly separated from the ceiling possibly due to horizontal displacement. The underwater entrance at -8 m depth connects to a chamber with terraces and sub-chambers. Dangerous to enter when seas are rough.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova del Dimoni is classified as a Site of Community Importance (SCI). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca there are a total of 30 cavities classified as SCI. At present (see date on the cave's data sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the required authorization from the Ministry of the Environment. Therefore, before making any visit, the required permit must be requested in order to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

Near the town of Porto Cristo beneath the cliffs lies this interesting cavity that has the peculiarity of being both a terrestrial and a marine cave. Famous among diving enthusiasts, the Cova del Dimoni is regularly visited by groups belonging to companies on the island that are dedicated to this activity.

The terrestrial entrance is easy to find as it is located almost at sea level in the cliff itself, at one end of a mini bay that is possibly an ancient cave that collapsed upon itself. The altitude of the entrance is approximately two meters by about three meters wide, with an orientation of 110°.

Once we enter the cavity, we immediately see that the cave continues through a narrow hole that we climb through to access a small vestibule about six meters long, slightly less wide, and never exceeding two meters in height. The concretions in this place give us an idea of what we are going to find in the cave, as some beautiful columns adorn the left side of the chamber, and the ceiling is covered with small stalactites, with some parietal flowstones also present. This is the area most affected by the soot produced by the massive use of candles that some thoughtless individuals use without any control when visiting the cave. I know that what I write will serve no purpose, but I hope it can prevent some people from bringing candles into the place, and if they do use them, to use the type that come encapsulated in small aluminum containers which, if removed after use, will cause insignificant damage — although the best option is the use of electric light.

To continue the visit, we must go along the left side of a small column from which we will descend approximately one meter to position ourselves on a sort of balcony from which we will continue the descent through a ramp, aided by the ropes that are usually installed. The floor is quite slippery, so one simply needs to be aware of it; otherwise there is nothing to fear.

Once the descent is finished, we will find ourselves at one end of a large chamber measuring approximately forty-one meters long by twenty-one meters wide and just over eight meters high from the water surface, flooded for the most part by a lake of brackish water since the sea enters through a siphon. The depth of the lake ranges from a few centimeters to six meters in the siphon area, although in most of the lake the depth is around two meters. The terrestrial visit of the chamber can be done using hand-line ropes installed in the cave, so we only need to make sure that said ropes are in good condition. Once past this zone, rich in parietal flowstones, we arrive at a place where, although the floor is quite irregular, one can walk without great difficulty — though watching where we step. What stands out most in this place is the great "column" that presides over the entire chamber. I have put it in quotes because it is currently separated from the ceiling by less than a meter, perhaps due to a solifluction process with horizontal displacement. Not far from the column, we will also find two small gours and some other mini pools, as the terrain descends until it meets the water level of the lake. If we want to visit the other terrestrial zones of the chamber, we will have to cross the lake, which I recommend because at the back of the chamber there is a small corner worth visiting, and another at the far left end of the chamber as we advance, hidden behind a wall that is nothing other than the ceiling level partially reaching the water level.

Basically, the cave consists of the entrance vestibule and the main chamber flooded by the lake for the most part, all adorned by a great column that presides over the main chamber, enhancing its beauty. The concretions of the Cova del Dimoni are reasonable, with pavement flowstones as well as parietal and ceiling flowstones, plus multiple columns, some of great beauty — the aforementioned one standing out above the rest — and some small gours in a couple of places in the cave.

As I noted at the beginning, the Cova del Dimoni has two entrances, one terrestrial and one marine. The marine entrance is at about eight meters depth, and communication between the two parts is through the siphon at the bottom of the lake in the great chamber, through which a beautiful turquoise-colored light penetrates.

From the website www.albatros-diving.com we have gathered the following information about the underwater part of this cave: it states that the underwater entrance at -8 meters depth is of sufficient width for several divers to enter simultaneously; we access a chamber of about 20 meters wide by 20 meters long, dotted with terraces at different levels and lateral sub-chambers, one of which hides the chimney that connects it to the lake of the terrestrial cave.

It is dangerous to enter the interior of this cave when the sea is rough, as the strong waves can make exiting difficult. Additionally, although we have not verified this, it is possible that the force of the waves pushes water through the siphon with great violence, which could make staying inside significantly dangerous.
